threats.title: Critical Fee Sweeping and Claim Denial of Service via Shared Position Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: The claim function incorrectly sweeps all accumulated fees for the entire shared Uniswap position instead of just the claiming user's portion, stealing fees from other users and permanently bricking subsequent claims due to underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract uses a single shared Uniswap V3 position for all investors. Calling pool.collect(max) retrieves all uncollected fees for the entire position, but the contract only credits the caller for their proportional share and sends the rest to the feeTaker. This leaves no fee tokens in the contract for subsequent claimers, causing their transactions to revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.collect(..., type(uint128).max) followed by transferring amountCollected0-amount0 to feeTaker

preconditions: Pool has launched successfully
Trading fees have accumulated in the Uniswap V3 pool
At least two users have vested liquidity to claim

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Sweeping max tokens from a shared position and sending the unallocated remainder to a fee taker
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after launch when fees have accumulated

operational_questions: Does the first claimer effectively steal all historical fees belonging to other users?
Will subsequent claimers consistently revert due to insufficient contract balance?
validation_requirements: Simulate two users claiming sequentially after fees accumulate
Verify contract token balances between claims

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for the ILO pool to launch and accumulate trading fees
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker (or any normal user) calls claim() for their vested position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Contract sweeps all fees, sends attacker their share, and sends the rest to feeTaker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All subsequent users attempt to claim, but the contract lacks the tokens to pay their calculated fee shares, resulting in reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - happens naturally during normal protocol operation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent lock of all remaining user liquidity and fees
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade or manual reimbursement
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All user claims after the first one

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: First to claim after fees accumulate

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed claim transactions with underflow or transfer errors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Fee taker receiving unexpectedly large fee transfers
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users unable to claim vested tokens

threats.title: Complete Whitelist Bypass via Logic Flaw in Index Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: A logical flaw in the checkWhitelist condition allows any unwhitelisted address to bypass the revert statement and purchase tokens freely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The condition `if (_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex)` evaluates to false for unwhitelisted users (who have an index of 0) whenever `_allowedWhitelistIndex` is greater than 0. This completely bypasses the intended security control.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Logical OR condition where 0 > allowedIndex evaluates to false, bypassing the revert

preconditions: Whitelist is active (_locked = false or bypassed via pool)
_allowedWhitelistIndex is set to a value > 0
Attacker address is not whitelisted (_whitelistIndex = 0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Whitelist validation logic uses an OR condition that fails open for default mapping values

operational_questions: Can an address with index 0 successfully execute a transfer from the pool?
Does the logic fail open in all cases where allowed index > 0?
validation_requirements: Test token transfer to an unmapped address during the whitelist phase

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe the _allowedWhitelistIndex being set to > 0 by the admin
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Use an entirely new, unwhitelisted wallet to interact with the Uniswap pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction succeeds because `0 > allowedIndex` is false, bypassing the revert

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete failure of the token launch whitelist mechanics
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires pausing or updating the whitelist contract
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token distribution and fair launch guarantees

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Purchases from addresses not in the whitelist mapping
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rapid token accumulation by unknown wallets
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Standard DEX buys succeeding despite whitelist restrictions

threats.title: Per-User Contribution Cap Bypass via NFT Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: Users can bypass the `maxCapPerUser` limit by transferring their position NFT to another wallet, resetting their local balance and allowing them to mint a new position with a fresh cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `buy` function checks `balanceOf(recipient) == 0` to determine if a new NFT should be minted. The cap is enforced against the specific NFT's `raiseAmount`. By transferring the NFT out, the user's balance becomes 0, allowing them to buy again and accumulate unlimited allocations up to the global hard cap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Cap enforced against _position.raiseAmount, but new positions are minted if balanceOf == 0

preconditions: User is whitelisted
Sale is active
Hard cap is not yet reached

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Cap enforcement is tied to a transferable asset rather than the user's address history

operational_questions: Does transferring the NFT reset the user's ability to buy?
Can a single whitelisted user drain the entire hard cap?
validation_requirements: Test buy -> transfer NFT -> buy sequence for the same whitelisted address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Whitelisted user buys the maximum allowed cap (maxCapPerUser)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User transfers the received ERC721 position NFT to a secondary wallet
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User calls buy() again from the original whitelisted address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract mints a new NFT and allows another maxCapPerUser purchase. Repeat until hard cap.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Whales can monopolize the ILO, destroying fair distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple position NFTs ending up in the same cold wallet
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Single whitelisted address executing multiple max-cap buys
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Buy followed immediately by NFT transfer

threats.title: Invalid Concentrated Liquidity Ranges due to Missing Upper Bound Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: The ILOManager fails to validate that the initial pool price is strictly less than the upper tick boundary, allowing the creation of pools with broken liquidity math.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `initILOPool`, the code checks `sqrtRatioLowerX96 < initialPoolPriceX96` but omits the check for `initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96`. If a project admin sets the upper tick below or equal to the initial price, the liquidity calculations in `_saleAmountNeeded` will yield incorrect results, potentially causing the launch to fail or mispricing the assets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96 && sqrtRatioLowerX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96, 'RANGE'); missing upper bound check

preconditions: Project admin initializes an ILO pool
Admin provides a tickUpper value that corresponds to a price <= initialPoolPriceX96

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Asymmetric range validation for concentrated liquidity bounds
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires project admin to set specific parameters

operational_questions: What happens to getLiquidity calculations when price >= upper bound?
Can this be used to intentionally brick a pool or extract value during launch?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with initial price > upper tick and trace liquidity math

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Malicious or mistaken project admin calls initILOPool with tickUpper <= initial price tick
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Validation passes because lower < initial and lower < upper
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users buy into the pool, but liquidity math calculates incorrect token ratios
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: At launch, addLiquidity fails or deploys highly skewed liquidity, causing loss of funds

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Failed pool launches or severely mispriced liquidity deployments
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires refunding users and abandoning the pool

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Pools initialized with unusual tick ranges
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatched liquidity ratios during buy phase

threats.title: Targeted Griefing of Whitelisted Allocations via Unrestricted Buy Recipients
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can consume a whitelisted user's allocation by calling `buy()` and specifying the victim as the recipient, preventing the victim from participating with their own funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `buy` function validates the whitelist status and cap limits against the `recipient` parameter, not `msg.sender`. Anyone can pay the `RAISE_TOKEN` to fill a whitelisted address's cap. While the attacker loses funds, they can effectively block targeted individuals (e.g., competitors, specific community members) from participating in highly anticipated ILOs.
preconditions: Target address is whitelisted
Target has not yet filled their maxCapPerUser
Attacker has sufficient RAISE_TOKEN

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Allowing third-party payments to consume limited allocation quotas without consent

operational_questions: Is there any mechanism for a user to reject an unwanted allocation?
Can this be used systematically to block key participants?
validation_requirements: Verify that msg.sender != recipient successfully consumes recipient's cap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify whitelisted addresses for a highly anticipated ILO
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls buy() specifying the target's address as recipient and maxCapPerUser as amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Target attempts to buy later but is rejected due to 'UC' (User Cap) error

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Community backlash, targeted exclusion of key investors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires admin to manually increase cap or whitelist a new address for the victim

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining they cannot buy despite not having participated
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Automated scripts filling caps of known addresses

threats.title: Permanent Claim Denial of Service via Zero-Duration Vesting Schedules
threats.hypothesis_description: Vesting schedules with identical start and end times cause a division-by-zero panic, permanently locking all user claims for that pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_validateVestSchedule` function checks that `schedule[i].start >= lastEnd` but does not enforce `schedule[i].end > schedule[i].start`. If a schedule is created with `start == end`, the `_unlockedLiquidity` calculation will attempt to divide by `(vest.end - vest.start) * BPS`, which evaluates to zero. This reverts the transaction, permanently locking funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS without ensuring end > start

preconditions: Project admin configures a vesting schedule with start == end
Pool launches successfully
User attempts to claim exactly at or after the start time

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing strict inequality validation for time-based denominators
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires project admin to submit flawed configuration

operational_questions: Does the validation logic permit start == end?
Does the division by zero permanently block all claims?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with zero-duration vest and attempt to claim

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Malicious or careless project admin initializes pool with a zero-duration vesting tier
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Investors buy into the pool and it launches
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Investors call claim() to retrieve their vested tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to division by zero, permanently locking the liquidity in the contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of investor funds and accumulated fees
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - funds are permanently stuck in the Uniswap position owned by the contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Pools initialized with start == end in vesting configs

threats.title: Unnecessary Fund Locking on Failed Soft Cap Raises
threats.hypothesis_description: If a sale ends without meeting the soft cap, investors cannot immediately reclaim their funds and must wait until the refund deadline, exposing them to unnecessary market risk.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `launch()` function reverts if `totalRaised < saleInfo.softCap`. However, the `claimRefund()` function requires `block.timestamp >= _project.refundDeadline`. If a sale concludes and clearly fails to meet the soft cap, there is no early termination path. Investors' funds remain locked in the contract until the refund deadline (default 7 days after launch time).

proof_of_signal.pattern: refundable modifier strictly requires block.timestamp >= _project.refundDeadline with no early exit for failed sales

preconditions: Sale end time has passed
Total raised is less than soft cap

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Lack of state transition for failed sales prior to the global refund deadline
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between sale end and refund deadline

operational_questions: Is there any operational workaround to refund users early?
Does the ILOManager owner have to manually intervene?
validation_requirements: Verify that refunds revert after sale end but before refund deadline

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Sale period ends, but soft cap is not met
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Investors attempt to refund but are blocked until the refund deadline, locking their capital

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Poor user experience, capital inefficiency, exposure to token volatility
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires waiting or admin changing the deadline

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed launch transactions, reverted refund transactions

threats.title: Whitelist Cap Bypass via Oracle Manipulation on New Pools
threats.hypothesis_description: For newly created Uniswap V3 pools, the TWAP oracle falls back to a very short observation window, allowing attackers to manipulate the spot price and bypass the ETH contribution cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `UniswapV3Oracle` uses `period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod`. If the pool was just created, `longestPeriod` might be only a few seconds. This effectively turns the TWAP into a spot price oracle. An attacker can manipulate the pool price in one transaction, drastically lowering the estimated ETH value of VULT, and then buy massive amounts of VULT bypassing the `_maxAddressCap`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Fallback to longestPeriod when PERIOD is unavailable, degrading TWAP to spot price

preconditions: VULT/ETH Uniswap V3 pool is newly created (observation history < 30 mins)
Whitelist is active
Attacker has capital to manipulate the pool price

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Oracle gracefully degrades to unsafe short periods instead of reverting
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must have set the oracle to a new pool

operational_questions: How quickly after pool creation does the whitelist sale begin?
Is the capital required for manipulation profitable compared to the bypassed cap?
validation_requirements: Simulate price manipulation on a pool with < 10 seconds of history and observe oracle output

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker monitors for the creation of the VULT/ETH pool and the start of the whitelist sale
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker executes a large swap to crash the VULT price in the pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: In the same or next block, attacker buys VULT through the whitelist mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Oracle returns the manipulated low price, calculating a tiny ETH contribution, allowing the attacker to buy far beyond the intended cap

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Whales bypass caps, draining the sale and ruining fair distribution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - cannot reverse token transfers easily

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Flash loan or large swap execution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large price swings in the Uniswap pool followed by massive whitelist purchases
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users exceeding the 3 ETH cap in actual token value
